coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of molecularly imprinted polymers based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots for specific detection of chlortetracycline by reversed phase microemulsion method
coredata.dc:creator: Ren, Yunyan
Fan, Zhefeng
coredata.dc:description: Among the tetracycline antibiotics, chlortetracycline (CTC) is the most frequently used antibiotic except for tetracycline (TC) for enhancing the ability of the organism to fight bacterial infections. The poor metabolism and degradability of CTC can cause serious health effects. Most studies have focused on the detection and analysis of TC, and research on CTC is relatively scarce. This is because the structures of CTC and TC and oxytetracycline (OTC) are extremely similar, and even indistinguishable. In this study, CTC was used as a template molecule and a molecularly imprinted layer was coated on the surface of highly fluorescent N-CDs using a reversed-phase microemulsion method to form N-CDs@MIPs. It was possible to specifically identify CTC without the influence of TC and OTC, which are extremely similar in structure. By comparing with the non-imprinted polymer (N-CDs@NIPs), it exhibited high sensitivity and selectivity with an imprinting factor of 2.02. And it was used in the determination of CTC in milk with recoveries and relative standard deviations of 96.7%–109.8% and 0.64%–3.27%, respectively, with high accuracy and precision. The specificity of the measurement is excellent compared with other assays, and it is a valid and reliable assay.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Molecularly imprinted polymers
Chlortetracycline
Specific identification

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst misctext primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of N-CDs, MIPs and NIPs
Analysis of the luminescence performance of MIPs
Exploring the mechanism of CTC burst MIPs
Optimizing the optimal temperature of the detection system
Stability and reusability study of MIPs
Quantification of CTC by MIPs and NIPs
Selectivity and anti-interference study of the detection system

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Mechanism diagram of CTC detection by MIPs.
SEM images of (A) MIPs and (B) NIPs; TEM plots of (C) N-CDs, the inset is a lattice image of N-CDs at the 5 nm scale; TEM images of (D) MIPs and (E) NIPs.
(A) FTIR spectra of N-CDs(a)/MIPs(b)/NIPs(c); (B) XRD plots of N-CDs/MIPs/NIPs.
(A) UV–vis spectrogram of CTC and the eluate for MIPs; (B) Fluorescence spectra of MIPs/MIPs bound CTC/NIPs/NIPs bound CTC/N-CDs/CTC.
(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of CTC (a) , fluorescence excitation spectrum (b) and emission spectrum (c) of N-CDs; (B) UV–vis absorption spectrum of CTC/MIPs/MIPs bound CTC.

Fluorescence spectra of (A) MIPs/(C) NIPs at CTC concentrations in the range of 6.67–111.33 μM; (B) linear relationship between CTC concentration and fluorescence change F0/F of MIPs system; (D) linear relationship between CTC concentration and fluorescence change F0/F of NIPs system. Change F0/F linearly.
(A) Selectivity study of MIPs and NIPs against CTC and their structural analogs; (B) Anti-interference ability study of MIPs and NIPs in the coexistence of CTC and interferers.
Structural formulae of TC, OTC and CTC.
Experimental results of MIPs for detection of CTC in milk.
Comparison with other methods for detecting CTC.

row.entry: Luminescent cellulose-based porous binary metal-organic gels

head.ce:title: Synthesis of molecularly imprinted polymers based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots for specific detection of chlortetracycline by reversed phase microemulsion method

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Science, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan, 030032, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Science, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan, 030032, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Among the tetracycline antibiotics, chlortetracycline (CTC) is the most frequently used antibiotic except for tetracycline (TC) for enhancing the ability of the organism to fight bacterial infections. The poor metabolism and degradability of CTC can cause serious health effects. Most studies have focused on the detection and analysis of TC, and research on CTC is relatively scarce. This is because the structures of CTC and TC and oxytetracycline (OTC) are extremely similar, and even indistinguishable. In this study, CTC was used as a template molecule and a molecularly imprinted layer was coated on the surface of highly fluorescent N-CDs using a reversed-phase microemulsion method to form N-CDs@MIPs. It was possible to specifically identify CTC without the influence of TC and OTC, which are extremely similar in structure. By comparing with the non-imprinted polymer (N-CDs@NIPs), it exhibited high sensitivity and selectivity with an imprinting factor of 2.02. And it was used in the determination of CTC in milk with recoveries and relative standard deviations of 96.7%–109.8% and 0.64%–3.27%, respectively, with high accuracy and precision. The specificity of the measurement is excellent compared with other assays, and it is a valid and reliable assay.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline antibiotics are a class of low-cost, broad-spectrum bacterial inhibitors [1]. The most frequently used of these are tetracycline (TC), chlortetracycline (CTC) and oxytetracycline (OTC), which are increasingly used in animal husbandry to enhance the ability of organisms to fight bacterial infections, promote animal growth and prevent animal diseases, and greatly reduce animal mortality [2]. However, the overuse and misuse of these antibiotics not only increases the amount of antibiotic residues in animal-derived foods, which are absorbed by humans through the food chain, leading to some serious consequences for human health, including allergic reactions, nephrotoxicity, gastrointestinal problems [3]. It also leads to a gradual increase in antibiotic resistance because of its own good water solubility going to transfer into the soil and sewage.

A large number of studies have been reported on the detection of TC, but there is a lack of reports on CTC [1,4–6]. Compared with TC, CTC also has low cost and excellent antimicrobial properties and is widely used as a veterinary drug for the prevention of animal diseases with great mobility and accumulation [7,8]. Only a small amount of CTC can be broken down in living organisms, and the vast majority is prone to accumulate in human and animal bodies, causing diseases such as hepatotoxicity, allergic reactions, bacterial resistance, gastrointestinal disorders, yellowing of teeth, and liver damage [9]. In addition, China has banned the addition of antibiotics in feeds completely since 2020 [7]. In this context, the detection of CTC faces a more critical situation.

CTC has a strong background interference and as result conventional detection methods are limited. The traditional methods for detecting CTC are mainly liquid chromatography, capillary electrophoresis, electrochemical analysis, colorimetric assays and enzyme-linked immunoassays [7]. These methods are limited by problems such as complex sample handling and complicated experimental operations as well as high costs, which do not allow for batch and rapid assays. Fluorescence analysis is widely used as an efficient detection method for its high sensitivity and simple pretreatment. Among them, carbon dots (CDs) have shown excellent performance in terms of non-toxicity, simple synthesis route, high chemical stability, good biocompatibility, environmental friendliness and unique photoluminescence. Therefore, they have been widely used for the development of fluorescent sensors targeting antibiotics [10,11]. Enhancing the performance of CDs by N-doping is one of the most commonly used methods to enhance fluorescence performance. For example, Zhang et al. used hawthorn to synthesize green nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs), and the excellent fluorescence performance of N-CDs can be used to detect CTC in pork sensitively and rapidly [12]. However, this method shows the same selectivity for structural analogs of CTC. In other words, its selectivity is not outstanding enough. Also, the detection process is susceptible to interference from other substances.

In contrast, molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) are special materials capable of specifically recognizing target molecules. MIPs are made by copolymerization of functional monomers and crosslinkers in the presence of a template molecule. After polymerization, the template molecule separates to obtain a polymer containing cavities that are complementary to the template molecule in shape, size and functional groups [13]. The advantages of MIPs include high selectivity, easy synthesis, high stability and low cost. MIPs lack output signals during analysis and detection despite their proper detection capabilities [14].

In recent years, MIPs based on carbon quantum dots (CDs@MIPs) have successfully combined the excellent selectivity of MIPs with the sensitivity of fluorescence analysis based on CDs. The synthesis of CDs@MIPs utilizes reversible chemical reactions. In the presence of a template molecule, functional monomers and crosslinkers are copolymerized to form complexes containing specific binding sites that are highly selective for target molecules with biological antibody-like properties. When the template is removed, CDs@MIPs are obtained, and imprinted cavities matching the shape, size, and functional groups of the target molecule are formed on the surface of the MIPs. When the template molecule reappears as the target to be tested, it is able to bind to the molecularly imprinted polymer, exhibiting desirable selectivity.

Moreover, the fluorescence burst of quantum dots appears because a new compound acquires the energy of quantum dots. And it occurs during the rebinding of CTC, formed between the –NH3 of CDs@MIPs and the –OH of CTC. The advantage of this design of fluorescent N-CDs@MIPs is the enhanced binding ability to specific targets. It also features the simplicity, high stability, excellent selectivity, short analysis time and low cost [10].

The commonly used methods for synthesizing N-CDs@MIPs are the reversed-phase microemulsion method and the sol-gel method. The core of the reversed-phase microemulsion method is the formation of a three-dimensional network structure in the form of a silica shell on the surface of carbon quantum dots after hydrolysis of the cross-linking agent. Internally, water is stabilized by a non-ionic surfactant and wrapped and dispersed in the microemulsion [15]. Then, a number of individual microdroplets are formed, which undergo polymerization reactions under the confining effect of the polar core of the reverse micelle. Compared to the sol-gel method, this method provides a simple way to generate stereospecific polymer spaces and greatly increases the solubility of the reactants. The prepared products were homogeneous in particle size with high porosity, smaller particle size, excellent physical rigidity, higher binding ability and faster mass transfer rate, which further improved the sensitivity of the prepared N-CDs@MIPs.

In this study, N-CDs contained MIPs were prepared by reversed-phase microemulsion method. A molecular fluorescence N-CDs@MIPs was developed for the specific identification of CTC. CTC itself has a distinct fluorescence emission spectrum at 612 nm, while the emission peak of N-CDs@MIPs is at 438 nm. Therefore, when N-CDs@MIPs and non-imprinted polymers (N-CDs@NIPs) detect CTC, two signals can be used to quantify the analytes to be detected (N-CDs@MIPs and N-CDs@NIPs are simply labelled as MIPs and NIPs for simplicity of writing and ease of remembering). The first signal is the change in the fluorescence intensity of MIPs. Because CTC is involved in the synthesis of MIPs, it is able to re-embed inside the blotting cavity, which in turn can burst the fluorescence of N-CDs [16]. The second signal is the intrinsic spectrum of CTC. Because the imprinted cavity inside the MIPs is certain, when CTC is added to the system, part of it will bind to the imprinted cavity inside the MIPs, and the remaining CTC will be free in the solution, and the intrinsic spectrum of CTC will appear. Compared with the previously reported [12], which used N-CDs sensors for CTC determination. The MIPs synthesized in this study significantly improved the selectivity of CTC and was applied to the determination of CTC in milk samples with satisfactory results.

Chlortetracycline (CTC) (AR, 98%), anhydrous citric acid, 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) (AR, 98%), acetonitrile, anhydrous ethanol, tetracycline (TC), oxytetracycline (OTC) were obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co. Cyclohexane, tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) AR, 98%), n-hexanol, Triton X-100, acetonitrile Shanghai Macklin Reagent. Ethylenediamine, acetone, erythromycin (ERY), chloramphenicol (CPL), streptomycin (SM), bovine serum albumin (BSA), tryptophan (Trp), leucine (Leu), Arginine(Arg), Serine (Ser), lysine (Lys), L-cysteine (L-Cys) and valine (Val) were obtained from Tianjin Comio Reagent Co. Arginine was purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Reagent Co. The water used in the whole experiment was deionized water.

Absorption spectra were measured on a TU-1901 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Pu-Analysis, China). The fluorescence emission signals of the materials were tested with an FS5 fluorescence/phosphorescence photometer (Edinburgh, UK). LGJ-12S freeze dryer (Songyuan Huaxing, China) was used for freeze drying of N-CDs. Materials were prepared with an MS7-Pro intelligent temperature-controlled magnetic stirrer (Selo Czech, USA). Infrared spectra were collected with an iS10 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR) (Nicolet, USA) to analyze the functional groups characteristic of the synthesized substances. UitimaIV-85 X-ray diffraction analyzer (XRD) (Rigaku, Japan) was used to analyze the materials by crystal plane diffraction. TG16-WS centrifuge (Xiang Yi Lu, China) was coupled with KH-300DE ultrasonic washing machine (Kunshan, China) was used to elute the MIPs. Carbon dots and polymer morphology were analyzed by Teenai G2 F20 transmission electron microscope (TEM) (FEI, USA) and SU2080 scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Hitachi, Japan), respectively DZF-6020 vacuum drying oven (Bo Xun, China) was used to dry the materials under vacuum. UPR-11-10TNP ultra-pure water machine (UPP, China) was used to prepare the deionized water required for the experiments. The pH of the assay system was optimized using a PHS-3E Digital pH Acidimeter (Yi Dian, Shanghai). The different temperatures required for testing were adjusted using the HH-2 digital thermostatic water bath (Guo Hua, Zhejiang). The particle size was calculated using Nano Measurer software to assess the particle size. Structural formulae of substances were drawn using Chemdraw. All data were visualized using Origin.

2.0 g of citric acid was added to a mixture of 1 mL of ethylenediamine and 40 mL of deionized water. It was heated at 150 °C for 2 h. After cooling, it was centrifuged and filtered in order to remove insoluble matter. The supernatant was collected and freeze-dried and prepared as a 10 mg/mL solution for use.

MIPs were synthesized by reversed-phase microemulsion method [17]. In a 100 mL flask, 15 mL of cyclohexane, 3.6 mL of hexanol, and 3.6 mL of Triton X-100 were added and stirred at 500 rpm/min for 20 min. Then, 2 mL of already prepared N-CDs were added and stirred for 30 min. 200 μL of TEOS and 200 μL of NH3·H2O were added and stirred airtight for 2 h. Finally, 20 μL of APTES and 10.3 mg CTC were added and stirred overnight. At the end of the reaction, 20 mL of acetone was used to break the emulsion, and the solid precipitate was collected by centrifugation at 8000 rpm/min for 10 min. To remove the template molecules and the unreacted N-CDs, the solid precipitate was washed repeatedly with an ethanol-acetonitrile (v/v, 8:2) solvent mixture, and the sign of cleanliness was that the CTC were not detectable in UV–vis spectra.
The non-molecularly imprinted polymers (NIPs) were prepared according to the above steps without the addition of the template molecule CTC.

Add 6.67–113.33 μmol/L CTC to the solution (200 μL 4 mg/mL MIPs), respectively, along with 100 μL of B–R pH = 5.0 buffer, and finally fix the volume to 3 mL. The incident slit and the exit slit were set to 5 nm and 3 nm, respectively, and a 400 nm filter was inserted. The fluorescence test was performed on the mixed solution at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm. On this basis, the optimal number of MIPs for detecting CTC as well as the optimal pH, reaction time and reaction temperature were also optimized.

The milk samples were processed with reference to the study reported in Ref. [18]. Three milk samples were purchased from the campus convenience store of Shanxi Normal University, China. Each milk sample was pretreated as follows. To precipitate proteins and lipids, 10% trichloroacetic acid and chloroform and milk were mixed in a mode of 2:1:1 by volume [19]. And the mixed solution was sonicated for 10 min and then centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min. Finally, filtration was performed with a 0.22 μm filter membrane [20]. In certain treated milk samples, spiked amounts were set so that the final concentrations of CTC were 0, 10, 20 and 30 μM. The CTC content in the treated milk samples was determined using the previously described method and procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of N-CDs, MIPs and NIPs
ce:section.ce:para: The N-CDs, MIPs and NIPs were prepared as shown in route A and B of Fig. 1 . The N-CDs containing a large number of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups were prepared by thermal decomposition of anhydrous citric acid and ethylenediamine. The microemulsion state is established by cyclohexane, n-hexanol, and Triton X-100. Among them, cyclohexane provides the organic solvent system, Triton X-100 is a surface activator, and n-hexanol is a co-surface activator. The MIPs were synthesized on the surface of N-CDs by reversed-phase microemulsion method using N-CDs as the fluorescent source, CTC as the template molecule, APTES as the functional monomer, TEOS as the crosslinker and NH3·H2O (25%)) as the catalyst. Among them, N-CDs are integrated into MIPs through the linkage between reactive groups to form a three-dimensional network cross-linked structure [21]. The template molecule CTC is eluted to give MIPs. In route B of Fig. 1, it can be clearly seen that CTC binds to APTES through hydrogen bonding, and elution can break the hydrogen bond and re-release the template molecule. When it meets CTC again, the two can recombine to burst the fluorescence of MIPs. MIPs and NIPs particles are formed by this non-covalent binding mode. The C route in Fig. 1 shows a clearer binding pattern in the form of stereospecific microspheres. Also, the specific recognition function of MIPs can be clearly seen. It can be concluded that the synthesized MIPs can specifically recognize CTC from structurally very similar CTC and OTC and TC.

The particle size and morphology of N-CDs, MIPs and NIPs were characterized by SEM and TEM. In the TEM image of N-CDs (Fig. 2 C), it was found that N-CDs are point-like particles with good dispersion, which have an average diameter of 6.95 nm (Fig. S1A). As shown in the SEM images of Fig. 2A and B, TEOS exerts a cross-linking effect, allowing MIPs and NIPs to exhibit a highly cross-linked morphology, ensuring their structural integrity and stability. Both have a smooth spherical appearance and are relatively uniformly dispersed. As seen in the TEM images of Fig. 2D and E, the MIPs and NIPs are uniform in size. Their average diameters are about 79.60 nm and 78.85 nm (Figs. S1B and S1C), respectively, which are both larger than the particle size of N-CDs, proving that N-CDs are completely encapsulated. It also indicates that MIPs and NIPs were successfully prepared. Especially in Fig. 2A, it was found that some of the MIPs particles showed a broken structure, which was due to the elution process that had to remove the template molecules, which caused some damage to the structure of the particles. On the other hand, it can be concluded that the synthesized particles are of core-shell structure, which is more favorable for the template molecules to function. It is also known that the polymer particles prepared using the reversed-phase microemulsion method are indeed more homogeneous than those prepared by the sol-gel method [18]. This can be visualized not only from the TEM plots but also from the particle size distribution plots Fig. S1B and Fig. S1C for MIPs and NIPs, which range from 76.40 nm to 83.60 nm and 76.05 nm–81.65 nm.

Fig. 3 A shows the characteristic functional groups of N-CDs (a), MIPs (b) and NIPs (c). The absorption peak at 3458 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of O–H/N–H. The peak at 2946 cm−1 is caused by the unsaturated stretching vibration of C–H. The strong signal at 1570 cm−1 is attributed to the backbone vibration peak of C–C on the benzene ring [22]. Peaks located at 1130 cm−1 (Si–O–Si asymmetric stretching vibration), 782 cm−1 (Si–O–Si symmetric stretching vibration) and 584 cm−1 (Si–O–Si bending vibration) indicate the presence of TEOS and APTES [13,23]. After elution, the FTIR spectra of MIPs were similar to NIPs, indicating that most of the template molecules had been removed, proving that the polymerization was successful and that the presence of CTC during the polymerization process did not disrupt the structure of the polymer. Moreover, it was confirmed that the synthesized particles are rich in amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface and have good water solubility, which improves the practical application.

The XRD spectra of N-CDs, MIPs and NIPs are shown in Fig. 3B. All three substances emerge from the peak at 2θ = 23.47°, corresponding to the diffraction plane of the carbon material (002), revealing the amorphous structure of all three [24]. It also shows that the structure of the N-CDs did not change during the synthesis of the polymers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the luminescence performance of MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–vis absorption spectra of the eluates of MIPs were studied as shown in Fig. 4 A. The elution of the initial polymer with ethanol/acetonitrile (8:2, v/v) solution shows that after five washes, CTC were barely detectable by UV–vis spectrophotometry. It indicates that the template molecule CTC were successfully eluted down. It also showed that there were almost no free fluorescent N-CDs in the MIPs solution, and they were firmly embedded in the blotted layer.

The fluorescence emission spectra at excitation wavelengths of 310 nm–360 nm is shown in Fig. S2. The optimal excitation wavelength of MIPs is 340 nm. At this time, the emission peaks of MIPs and NIPs are at 438 nm, which shows a 9 nm blue shift compared with the emission wavelength of N-CDs at 447 nm (Fig. 4B). This is due to the formation of core-shell structures in MIPs and NIPs [25].

As shown in Fig. 4B. On the one hand, CTC shows emission peaks at 612 nm, and the peaks at 597 nm can be inferred to be free CTC in the system when MIPs and NIPs are bound to CTC, respectively. Meanwhile, it can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of MIPs is stronger than that of NIPs at the same concentration. This is because the MIPs have formed bare cavities on the surface of the core-shell structure after the embedding and elution of the template molecules, which makes the fluorescence source partially exposed [9]. In addition, the excitation spectra of the MIPs and NIPs can be seen in Fig. S5, where they are both presented in a normalised form.

On the other hand, the fluorescence of both MIPs and NIPs showed different degrees of attenuation when CTC were added. This is because MIPs binding to CTC is specific binding. Whereas NIPs binding CTC is non-specific binding, in other words, physisorption [26,27].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploring the mechanism of CTC burst MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: The polymer particle size distribution prepared by the reverse microemulsion method is more uniform compared to the sol-gel method. The template molecule CTC polymerizes with APTES, and its surface is rich in nitrogen and oxygen single electron pairs that combine to form hydrogen bonds and van der Waals forces [28]. Then, the complexes were formed by wrapping on N-CDs in the presence of cross-linker TEOS and catalyst NH3·H2O. After elution of CTC with ethanol and acetonitrile, MIPs with specific recognition of functional cavities are formed. When MIPs bind again to CTC, a significant burst phenomenon occurs.

Usually, the target molecule can absorb the fluorescence of the quantum dot once the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the molecule to be measured overlaps with the fluorescence spectrum of the quantum dot [28]. The fluorescence burst may be attributed to the internal filtration effect (IFE), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), and electron transfer [29]. As shown in Fig. 5 A, CTC has a clear UV–vis absorption in the 200 nm–450 nm range. Although there is no overlap between its emission spectrum and that of N-CD, there is a clear overlap between it and the excitation spectrum of the N-CDs [30]. This indicates that N-CDs did not transfer energy to CTC [31]. It can be tentatively concluded that IFE or charge transfer induced the fluorescence burst of MIPs.

The above inference is further verified. The UV–vis absorption spectra of CTC and MIPs bound CTC are verified in Fig. 5B for comparison. There are no obvious absorption peaks for the MIPs. However, the faint absorption peaks that appear after the MIPs bound CTC are the peaks at 236 nm, 276 nm and 373 nm respectively. These three peaks are very close to the absorption peaks of CTC. In other words, there was little change in the position of the characteristic absorption peaks of CTC and MIPs bound CTC. In this way, it can be assumed that no complexes were formed after the addition of CTC to the MIPs. Therefore, there was no electron transfer between CTC and N-CDs. Therefore, in the presence of CTC, the main mechanism of fluorescence burst of N-CDs is presumed to be IFE. Unlike other mechanisms, IFE requires neither energy transfer nor electron transfer, which is very simple and flexible and has received much attention in recent years. This mechanism of action also provides a fast, sensitive, simple and highly selective platform for the detection of CTC. It provides a new idea for the rapid and sensitive detection of antibiotics in food [7].
After the synthesis of MIPs and template removal, the materials were tested to obtain the best burst conditions.

To obtain the optimal interaction between CTC and MIPs , the optimal pH was studied. 200 μL MIPs solution with 200 μL 1 mM CTC solution at a fixed volume of 3.0 mL. Since CTC exhibit strong intrinsic fluorescence under alkaline conditions, a B–R buffer solution with pH 3.0–7.0 was chosen for fluorescence testing as shown in Fig. S3A. The best burst value was observed when the pH was fixed at 5.0. The pH = 5.0 was finally chosen as the best pH for the system.

The fluorescence changes of 160 μL–280 μL of 4 mg/mL MIPs mixed with 200 μL of 1 mM CTC at pH = 5.0 was tested. As shown in Fig. S3B, the best burst effect was observed when the volume of MIPs was 200 μL.

Preliminary fluorescence burst experiments showed that the measurement accuracy was poor when mixing CTC and MIPs solutions. This is because a certain time is required for effective recognition interactions between CTC and MIPs [32]. Therefore, tests were performed at pH = 5.0 using 200 μL MIPs solution and 1 mM CTC, and fluorescence was recorded at mixed solution interaction times of 0–3.5 min. The results in Fig. S3C, show that the smoothing value was reached within 1 min and the fluorescence signal was kept stable afterwards. Therefore, a reaction time of 1 min was finally chosen for the next experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimizing the optimal temperature of the detection system
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence intensity of 200 μL 4 mg/mL MIPs solution and 200 μL 1 mM CTC solution was recorded at different temperatures (15–45 °C) under pH = 5.0 conditions. The optimal temperatures are shown in Fig. S3D. The fluorescence intensity of MIPs showed strong stability at different temperatures. Therefore, room temperature was chosen as the best temperature for the next experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and reusability study of MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of MIPs was investigated in Figs. S3E and S3F. The fluorescence intensity ratio (F0/F) of MIPs was measured repeatedly every 10 min under 365 nm UV light irradiation to estimate the fluorescence stability of MIPs. 92.64% of the initial value of the fluorescence intensity of MIPs after 7 measurements in 60 min indicated that the fluorescence stability of MIPs was excellent (Fig. S3E). And the effect of ionic strength on the test system was tested, and in Fig. S3F, it is seen that the ions on the surface of MIPs aggregated within the NaCl concentration of 0–300 mM, but did not affect the specific interactions such as hydrogen bonding and ionic interactions between MIPs and CTC [33]. Therefore, in a solution of certain ionic strength, MIPs can detect CTC normally.

The reusability of MIPs was studied in a system containing 200 μL of 4 mg/mL MIPs solution and 200 μL of 1 mM CTC solution at pH = 5.0 and a response time of 1 min. After each assay, the solid pellets were collected by centrifugation and washed with ethanol/acetonitrile (8:2 v/v) to remove the template molecules CTC, and the recovered MIPs were bound to CTC again to test for changes in fluorescence performance. The number of repeated experiments on the recovered MIPs with CTC and the recovery efficiency (F/F0) of the MIPs (F0 refers to the initial fluorescence intensity of the MIPs and F refers to the fluorescence intensity value of the recovered MIPs) are shown in Fig. S4. The recovery efficiency of the MIPs reached 90.82% after four cycles and 85.24% on the fifth. In other words, this MIPs can be reused at least 4 times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of CTC by MIPs and NIPs
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence spectra were recorded for different concentrations of CTC reacting with quantitative MIPs and NIPs particles. CTC itself has a distinct fluorescence emission spectrum at 612 nm (as shown in Fig. 4B), while the emission spectra of MIPs and NIPs are at 438 nm. The results in Fig. 6 A and C indicate that as the CTC concentration increases, the decrease of fluorescence intensity at 438 nm for MIPs is much larger than that for NIPs. Also, it can be seen in Fig. 6A and C that the enhancement of the signal value at 597 nm in the MIPs system is much weaker than that in the NIPs system, and the enhancement of the signal values was much weaker than that of the NIPs system.

This is because CTC was involved in the synthesis of MIPs. The blotting cavity inside the MIPs is certain, and when CTC was added to the system, most of them will bind to the blotting cavity inside the MIPs, and a small number of CTC will be free in the solution, and the fluorescence emission spectra of CTC appear. Therefore, as the concentration of CTC increases, the amount of CTC free outside the system also increases gradually.

While CTC was not involved in the synthesis of NIPs, there was no blotting cavity. This resulted in a small amount of CTC binding non-specifically to NIPs, while a large amount of CTC was free in the solution. With the increase of CTC concentration, the enhancement of fluorescence signal at 438 nm was weaker than that at 597 nm.

The linearity, accuracy, precision and limit of detection (LOD) of MIPs were determined under optimal conditions. The fluorescence burst properties of MIPs follow the Stern-Volmer formula: F0/F = 1+KSV[CTC], where F0 and F are the MIPs before and after the addition of CTC fluorescence intensity, KSV is the Stern-Volmer burst constant, and [CTC] is the concentration of CTC [34]. In the concentration range of 6.67–111.33 μM, the linear relationships for MIPs and NIPs (as in Fig. 6B and D) were F
0
/F = 0.0266*[CTC]+0.9935 (R2 = 0.9981), F
0
/F = 0.0132*[CTC]+0.8971 (R2 = 0.9957).

The detection limit for CTC detection by MIPs was calculated based on LOD = 3δ/S [34]. Where δ is the standard deviation of the fluorescence intensity ratio (F0/F) in the absence of CTC presence (n = 11) and S is the slope of the linear relationship between F0/F and CTC concentration. Accordingly, LOD = 3.19 μM.

The imprinting factor (IF), defined as the ratio of KSV of MIPs to KSV of NIPs, was used to evaluate the selectivity of MIPs [35]. The calculated blotting factor for CTC recognition by MIPs was 2.02. This indicates that this MIPs has a high selectivity for CTC.
In summary, the synthesized MIPs can be used for the effective quantitative analysis of trace CTC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference study of the detection system
ce:section.ce:para: The specific selection ability of MIPs was verified by comparing five structural analogs of CTC and seven amino acids commonly found in bovine serum albumin and milk. The experimental results showed (Fig. 7 A) that CTC had the best burst effect when interacting with MIPs, and it could be found that TC and OTC also showed significant burst, but the burst performance was much inferior to that of CTC. This indicates that the structures of TC and OTC are extremely similar to CTC.

And this can be verified by the structures of the three (as in Fig. 8 ). CTC, OTC and TC belong to the same TC family and they have similar chemical structures. The main difference is that CTC contains a Cl atom, so only the π-π conjugation effect occurs between CTC and MIPs, leading to a strong interaction between the both [8]. Studied from another perspective, TC and OTC contain hydroxyl groups similar to CTC, but the recognition sites of MIPs imprinted cavities are not fully complementary to them and cannot form hydrogen bonds, leading to a smaller fluorescence burst. As a comparison, NIPs do not have selective recognition cavities and rely on their own physical adsorption only to bind a small number of analytes without significant selectivity for CTC. These results indicate that the effective recognition sites of the prepared MIPs have good recognition ability for CTC.

Here, the blotting effect can also be evaluated using the IF (Imprinting factor), which indicates the strength of the binding ability of MIPs to CTC and structural analogs. IF is the ratio of Ksv of MIPs to Ksv of NIPs. Ksv was calculated using the fluorescence burst equation Stern-Volmer formula: F0/F = 1 + KSV [Object] [34]. Where F0/F is the amount of change in fluorescence signal when MIPs and NIPs interact with different objects (the highest point of the histogram in Fig. 7 (A)). [Object] is the concentration of CTC and structural analogs, both fixed at 66.67 μM. The IF values for CTC, TC and OTC binding to MIPs in Fig. 7 (A) were calculated in Table S1. A comparison of the tables leads to the conclusion that MIPs have the highest blotting factor for interaction with CTC, at 2.67. In other words, MIPs have the highest ability to selectively recognize CTC.

Resistance to common interfering ions and amino acids during the detection of CTC by MIPs and NIPs was studied as shown in Fig. 7B. CTC (1 mM) solutions with excess interfering substances (concentration 5 times the CTC concentration) present were prepared. The interference of NO3−, SO4 2−, K+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, SO3 2−, Cu2+, Fe2+ and interfering substances such as Arg, Ser, Lys, L-Cys, Val, Leu. with the detection process was investigated in competitive binding experiments. In the presence of various interferents, no significant changes in F0/F. It was demonstrated that MIPs could efficiently detect CTC without interference from common substances.

The developed MIPs were used to detect CTC in milk, and different volumes of CTC (final concentrations of 0, 10, 20 and 30 μM, respectively) were added to the treated milk samples as spiked amounts. As shown in Table 1 , the recoveries and relative standard deviations of CTC in milk were measured in the range of 96.7%–109.8% and 0.64%–3.27%, respectively. These results indicated that the prepared fluorescent molecular fluorescence MIPs were highly accurate and precise for the detection of chlortetracycline in milk, which is an effective and reliable detection tool.

The advantageous items of this method compared to other means of detecting CTC are clearly visible in Table 2 . MIPs particles with high sensitivity and selectivity can be synthesized using only cheap and efficient N-CDs and some low-cost chemical reagents. The introduction of the imprinting technique resulted in optically stable MIPs, avoided complicated pretreatment, shortened response time, and also provided excellent anti-interference ability. Most importantly this method exhibits a unique and specific identification ability. Moreover, this work shows the advantages of short response times and a wide linear range compared to similar work. The above arguments reflect that MIPs are feasible and reliable in detecting CTC.

In this study, MIPs particles for specific detection of CTC was developed by forming a molecularly imprinted layer with a core-shell structure indicated by N-CDs by a reversed-phase microemulsion method. The particle combines both the high sensitivity of N-CDs and the high selectivity of molecular blotting technique, and is a new detection strategy for tetracycline antibiotic residues that can specifically identify CTC from CTC, TC, and OTC with extremely similar structures. The particle was applied to the determination of CTC in milk samples and satisfactory results were obtained. However, further work can be carried out to explore the potential use of this strategy for the detection of other foods, pesticides and other structural analogs for analysis.
Yunyan Ren – conceptualising, data acquisition, writing of first draft, formal analysis, manuscript editing. Zhefeng Fan – Supervision, manuscript editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2023.124898.
